EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying low pressure system, the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole, moved northward along the east coast Thursday, September 30th and Friday, October 1st, bringing very heavy rains to east central New York. Abundant tropical moisture was transported into the region. Storm total rainfall of 3 to 9 inches occurred, resulting in widespread river and small stream and urban flooding, including water in basements.||Prior to this event, rainfall deficits were running around 6 to 9 inches across east central New York.||In the City of Albany, a roof collapse was reported on a Morton Avenue home due to the heavy rainfall, with the time of occurrence unknown. ||In Leeds, in Greene County, a 62-year old man drove past barriers and emergency personnel at Cauterskill Road and County Route 23B and into a flooded area. The force of the water caused his vehicle to flip and swept it 150 yards until it became stuck against some trees. The man was rescued and charged with reckless endangerment along with several vehicle and traffic violations. ||In addition, the combination of an intensifying low pressure system and high pressure off the New England coast created a very strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong and gusty southerly winds occurred across east central New York as the low approached. The strongest winds were across the Taconics into Washington County, where gusts up to 50 to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred on the Esopus Creek at Cold Brook. Cold Brook crested at 18.86 feet at 0600 LST on October 1st. Flood stage is 11 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system interacting with abundant tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole dropped 3 to 6 inches of rain across central New York. Steady and occasionally heavy rain fell across much of the region,  causing flash flood problems, as well as minor flooding of the larger main stem rivers. The rain and flooding continued into the overnight and next day (October 1), especially across parts of the Catskills. Higher amounts of rain fell across Delaware county in the western Catskills, where 6 to 8 inches of rain caused more serious flood problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in a few areas of Newark Valley. The most serious flooding occurred where water and debris flowed downhill across Rock Street to Irishtown Road. Both roads were closed due to flooding. The flood waters and debris flowed across Rock Street to Slosson Creek, which overflowed. Water continued across Route 38 in the Village of Newark Valley. Homes were flooded along Mill Street and Clinton Street where the creek overflowed. Elsewhere in the village, the Owego Creek flooded the Trout Ponds recreation area. Flash flooding also occurred along Route 38B in the town of Newark Valley due to a mud slide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported in Accord during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the region from the west during the day Monday, September 13th, while a disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved over the region. Isolated severe thunderstorms occurred across Warren County and portions of the eastern Catskills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail was reported in Marlboro during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large closed upper atmospheric area of low pressure remained quasi-stationary across New England and Quebec from December 5th through December 8th. A relatively strong surface storm located across New Brunswick on December 6th retrograded across Quebec on December 7th.||A broad, deep layered and moisture laden north to northeast flow delivered snow across much of Vermont and portions of northern New York on the 6th and 7th. Local north-northwest facing mountain upslope regions provided local enhancement that accounted for wide variety in snowfall amounts.||Snowfall amounts in the Champlain Valley ranged from a trace to several inches, 2 to 6 inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley with higher amounts in the Adirondacks and localized much higher amounts in those favorable upslope locations. some of the highest amounts included; 20 inches in Lake Placid (Essex county), 15 inches in Hannawa Falls (St. Lawrence county), 14 inches in Malone (Franklin county) and 12 inches at Lyon Mountain (Clinton county).

An intensifying low pressure system, the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole, moved northward along the east coast Thursday, September 30th and Friday, October 1st, bringing very heavy rains to east central New York. Abundant tropical moisture was transported into the region. Storm total rainfall of 3 to 9 inches occurred, resulting in widespread river and small stream and urban flooding, including water in basements.||Prior to this event, rainfall deficits were running around 6 to 9 inches across east central New York.||In the City of Albany, a roof collapse was reported on a Morton Avenue home due to the heavy rainfall, with the time of occurrence unknown. ||In Leeds, in Greene County, a 62-year old man drove past barriers and emergency personnel at Cauterskill Road and County Route 23B and into a flooded area. The force of the water caused his vehicle to flip and swept it 150 yards until it became stuck against some trees. The man was rescued and charged with reckless endangerment along with several vehicle and traffic violations. ||In addition, the combination of an intensifying low pressure system and high pressure off the New England coast created a very strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong and gusty southerly winds occurred across east central New York as the low approached. The strongest winds were across the Taconics into Washington County, where gusts up to 50 to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains and the Mohawk River topping the flood wall at the New York State Canal Corporation facility on Route 30A in Fonda flooded part of the track and infield at the Fonda Speedway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary cold front ignited strong to severe thunderstorms across the Tri-State area.
A nearly stationary cold front ignited strong to severe thunderstorms across the Tri-State area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was reported to have been struck by lightning while walking to his car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two day period of strong northeast winds developed across the region between strong high pressure to the northwest of the area and weak low pressure tracking up the coast.  The resultant tidal piling caused widespread minor and isolated moderate coastal flooding along Western Long Island Sound, New York Harbor, and the south shore bays of Nassau and Queens Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS gauge at Freeport exceeded its moderate flood stage of 5.8 feet MLLW at 5:00 pm (EDT), peaking at 6.14 feet MLLW at 5:42 pm (EDT).  During this time, significant street flooding was observed on Hudson Avenue in Freeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two day period of strong northeast winds developed across the region between strong high pressure to the northwest of the area and weak low pressure tracking up the coast.  The resultant tidal piling caused widespread minor and isolated moderate coastal flooding along Western Long Island Sound, New York Harbor, and the south shore bays of Nassau and Queens Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some minor coastal flooding was reported in the area of Rockaway Boulevard and Brookville Boulevard near John F. Kennedy International Airport, as a few vehicles were stuck in flood waters between 5:30 pm and 6:00 pm (EDT).  Three calls were placed to 911 for assistance.  No injuries were reported and the coastal flooding resulted in a temporary road closure until the waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Canadian high pressure accompanied by dry air and clear skies allowed temperatures to fall below freezing in areas well away from the Great Lakes. Low temperatures recorded included: Lowville 26, Watertown 31, Whitesville 25 and Limestone 29 degrees.
A strong low pressure system moved across the region and passed to the northeast.  It produced wind gusts from around 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 50 mph was measured at Shinnecock Hills at 8:29 pm.  Shirley Airport measured a wind gust to 46 mph, as did Napeague during the late morning and early afternoon.

On September 30th, an area of low pressure moved north through the mid Atlantic states. Southerly flow ahead of the low tapped into deep tropical moisture, including moisture associated with the dissipated remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole.||Heavy rain spread into Saint Lawrence and Franklin Counties during the afternoon and early evening and eventually deposited 3 to 4 inches of rain across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding in Whippleville, Malone, and Hogansburg. In Whippleville, Homes were surrounded by water near county Route 25 and required evacuation. In Malone, Moses street had water over the road and basements were flooded. Basement flooding was reported as well in Hogansburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 30th, an area of low pressure moved north through the mid Atlantic states. Southerly flow ahead of the low tapped into deep tropical moisture, including moisture associated with the dissipated remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole.||Heavy rain spread into Saint Lawrence and Franklin Counties during the afternoon and early evening and eventually deposited 3 to 4 inches of rain across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding in Canton, Ogdensburg, and Potsdam. In Canton, New York State 11 was closed between Routes 310 and 68 by high water. Thirty five to forty homes in Ogdensburg had basement flooding, and there were multiple reports of city street flooding. In Potsdam water inundated the first floor of a home, and there was extensive flooding of streets, yards, and basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some minor flooding occurred in the upper Mohawk Valley on Thursday, October 7th. Heavy rain fell across the far headwaters of the Mohawk River above Lake Delta. High water on the upper Mohawk between Rome, NY and Utica, NY forced the Erie-Mohawk Canal from Lock E2 to E22 to remain closed until Saturday, October 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Erie Mohawk Canal was shut down due to high water along its length from Lock 2 (Waterford, NY) to Lock 22 (Rome, NY).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system interacting with abundant tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole dropped 3 to 6 inches of rain across central New York. Steady and occasionally heavy rain fell across much of the region,  causing flash flood problems, as well as minor to moderate flooding of the larger main stem rivers. The rain and flooding began on September 30 and continued into the overnight and next day (October 1), especially across parts of the Catskills. Higher amounts of rain fell across Delaware county in the western Catskills, where 6 to 8 inches of rain caused more serious flood problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded, and at least one home was evacuated.

The body of the 55 year old woman was found the next morning along the shore of the Neversink Reservoir.

Storm total rainfall of 3 to 9 inches occurred, resulting in widespread river and small stream and urban flooding, including water in basements.||Prior to this event, rainfall deficits were running around 6 to 9 inches across east central New York.||In the City of Albany, a roof collapse was reported on a Morton Avenue home due to the heavy rainfall, with the time of occurrence unknown. ||In Leeds, in Greene County, a 62-year old man drove past barriers and emergency personnel at Cauterskill Road and County Route 23B and into a flooded area. The force of the water caused his vehicle to flip and swept it 150 yards until it became stuck against some trees. The man was rescued and charged with reckless endangerment along with several vehicle and traffic violations. ||In addition, the combination of an intensifying low pressure system and high pressure off the New England coast created a very strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong and gusty southerly winds occurred across east central New York as the low approached. The strongest winds were across the Taconics into Washington County, where gusts up to 50 to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Western Avenue was reported closed due to flooding from Schoolhouse Road to Fuller Road (near Stuyvesant Plaza) in Guilderland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a surface cold front that was dropping southeast through the Great Lakes region. An upper level low across eastern Ontario and western Quebec moved east during the afternoon. This upper level low spread cooler temperatures aloft and an area of enhanced mid-level winds with this cold front. This aided in severe thunderstorm development with both large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stretching from the Great Lakes to the Central Plains moved east during the day as low pressure tracked into eastern Canada. The cold front moved into a very warm and humid air mass in place across central New York, with dew points in the low to mid 70s. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the area, and several of the storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down. A roof was partially blown off a small shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Randolph in Chautauqua County at 525 pm EDT.  The initial touchdown occurred just south of Interstate 86 between exits 15 and 16. The tornado crossed Interstate 86 and damaged a gas station near exit 16���pulling the pumps out of the ground.  The tornado continued across the Village of Randolph at its maximum size and intensity. The entire Village of Randolph lost power. About a dozen homes sustained significant structural damage in the village. The tornado continued for a total distance of about seven miles. Large trees were felled. Several automobiles were destroyed by falling trees. The final damage from the tornado appeared along County Rte 394 near exit 17 off I-86 in the town of Cold Spring. A NWS survey concluded an EF2 rating with winds estimated to 125 mph. The county emergency manager estimated damage at $7 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey confirmed an EF2 tornado touched down.

Four homes and one business had damage to roofs from fallen trees. Two structures showed minor wind damage to roofs. Winds were estimated at 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stretching from the Great Lakes to the Central Plains moved east during the day as low pressure tracked into eastern Canada. The cold front moved into a very warm and humid air mass in place across central New York, with dew points in the low to mid 70s. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the area, and several of the storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down just southeast of Potter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front tracking through the region produced severe thunderstorms across the region, including a tornado in the Bronx.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the grounds of the Hebrew Home for the Aged along the east bank of the Hudson River west of Palisade Avenue in Riverdale. High winds flattened a flag pole, downed trees and snapped and sheared off many tree tops. A few trees crashed through first floor windows that caused them to shatter resulting in 7 minor injuries. On the south side of the building, large trees were totally stripped of their canopies and one became a projectile as it was wedged between an upstream tree. An eyewitness account at this location stated that he saw funnel clouds develop upstream over the Hudson as the thunderstorm approached.||Several eyewitnesses along the southeast path of this storm stated that they saw clouds and debris swirl in white out conditions as many tree tops were snapped and sheared off. Significant damage occurred along West 254th Street northwest across Netherlands and Arlington Streets, where at least one residential roof was damaged west of 254th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the western southern tier in a warm, humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds which downed trees in parts of Cattaraugus and Allegany counties. In Freedom, a thunderstorm microburst flattened 15 to 20 acres of hardwood forest. Several of the exposed root balls were fifteen to twenty feet tall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An aerial survey flown by the New York State police confirmed straighline winds from a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed over Lake George during the morning of Saturday, July 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public submitted pictures of a funnel cloud over Lake George.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the higher terrain of the western Catskills as an upper level disturbance moved through the Saint Lawrence River valley. One slow moving cluster of thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall over north central Sullivan county early in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar indicated a basin average rainfall of almost 4 inches within 2 hours over the Spring Brook watershed.  The heaviest rainfall was confined to the middle portion of the basin with locally heavier rainfall of 6 to 8 inches.  One rain gauge had 5 inches of rain in this portion of the Spring Brook watershed.  Evacuations took place along Spring Brook and were precautionary in nature.  Water over-topped a minimum of three bridges along State Route 206 in the Spring Brook watershed and one along the Little Spring Brook tributary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved eastward across New York State, paralleling Interstate 90, into New England on Sunday, June 6th. The air mass ahead of the system's cold front was warm and unstable. Thunderstorms preceded and accompanied the cold front as it moved across the area during the late morning through the mid afternoon hours across east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down and fell through a house in Delmar due to strong thunderstorm winds. Two people were home, but no one was injuried.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved northeastward toward northern Pennsylvania, and through the region on the 25th. A surge of deep moisture associated with this system produced a period of heavy rain from late on the 24th, through the morning hours on the 25th. In addition, mild temperatures combined with the rain to melt an existing snow-pack to cause isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed several roads and bridges in the Canisteo area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved northeastward toward northern Pennsylvania, and through the region on the 25th. In addition, mild temperatures combined with the rain to melt an existing snow-pack to cause isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded near Rome. Route 365 between Rome and Floyd was covered by about 8 inches of water over a small section.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread showers and thunderstorms across central New York. Several of the storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and limbs were  blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and strong upper level trough moved across the Tri-State, triggering severe thunderstorms across Southeast New York during the afternoon.  This included both supercells and squall lines, producing severe winds and hail across the Lower Hudson Valley, New York City, and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported on Hyman Drive in Farmingdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and embedded thunderstorms dropped upwards of an inch of rain in less than an hour over the hilly terrain on the west side of Canandaigua Lake. This resulted in a mudslide which sent water, dirt and debris flowing across West Lake Road. A portion of the road was closed as crews cleaned up mud, broken trees limbs and debris. The flow damaged several patios on the lake side of the road as mud covered patios, driveways and boathouses.

This aided in severe thunderstorm development with both large hail and damaging winds.

Many of the storms that developed produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal boundary and upper level disturbance moved across the region during the late afternoon and evening hours of Saturday, July 17th. With an unstable airmass in place, several clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed and moved across east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Gloversville due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front crossing the Tri-State spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across all of southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported down in the town of Tappan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the Tri-State Area triggered thunderstorms, which produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding across portions of Nassau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Meadow, a car was stranded in approximately 2 feet of water on Hempstead Turnpike (Westbound) at Carman Avenue.  This intersection was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extending through the Tri-State Area from a low pressure system moving across the northern Mid-Atlantic States acted as the focus for heavy rain and training thunderstorms in Queens and Nassau Counties.  Slow moving showers with embedded thunderstorms produced heavy rain which led to flash flooding on Long Island.  In addition, a woman driving in Orange County lost control of her car in a heavy shower, killing both herself and her unborn child.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of water accumulated at the intersection of Floral Blvd. and Daisy Ave. in Floral Park, making the road impassable.  A Skywarn Spotter in Floral Park reported 3.82 inches of total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifted north from West Virginia to Quebec. The low brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the eastern Lake Ontario counties. The thunderstorm winds gusted to 65 mph. Trees were downed near Redfield, Rector, Lowville and Sperryville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying low pressure system, the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole, moved northward along the east coast Thursday, September 30th and Friday, October 1st, bringing very heavy rains to east central New York. Abundant tropical moisture was transported into the region. Storm total rainfall of 3 to 9 inches occurred, resulting in widespread river and small stream and urban flooding, including water in basements.||Prior to this event, rainfall deficits were running around 6 to 9 inches across east central New York.||In the City of Albany, a roof collapse was reported on a Morton Avenue home due to the heavy rainfall, with the time of occurrence unknown. ||In Leeds, in Greene County, a 62-year old man drove past barriers and emergency personnel at Cauterskill Road and County Route 23B and into a flooded area. The force of the water caused his vehicle to flip and swept it 150 yards until it became stuck against some trees. The man was rescued and charged with reckless endangerment along with several vehicle and traffic violations. ||In addition, the combination of an intensifying low pressure system and high pressure off the New England coast created a very strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong and gusty southerly winds occurred across east central New York as the low approached. The strongest winds were across the Taconics into Washington County, where gusts up to 50 to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 214, Main Street, High Street, Station Road and Plank Road, all near the Esopus Creek, were reported closed due flooding in Phoenicia. The Bridge Street bridge in Phoenicia suffered structural damage and was also closed. ||Fifteen to 20 homes had to be evacuated in Phoenicia due to the flooding.  In addition, Onteora school district canceled classes for the day.||States of Emergency were declared in the towns of Shandaken and Hardenburgh due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the east coast and interacted with a stalled frontal boundary and approaching upper level low pressure system.  Strong southerly flow allowed for the transport of tropical moisture, including the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole, up the coast which resulted in heavy rain and flooding in portions of New York City, Nassau and Rockland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The left and right lanes on the Belt Pkwy. were closed due to flooding from JFK Airport to the Brooklyn-Queens County line.  Total rainfall recorded by the ASOS at JFK Airport and LaGuardia Airport were 3.08 inches and 3.06 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Olsen Road in Oppenheim due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, coupled with an approaching upper level trough caused severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down on Manor Lane just west of Jamesport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, coupled with an approaching upper level trough caused severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph was reported at Cedar Beach.

Many of the storms that developed produced large hail and damaging winds.

A low pressure system moved eastward across east central New York on Wednesday, July 21st. The system's warm front lifted to the north during the morning, ushering in a moist and unstable airmass. A cold front moved across the area during the evening. In addition, a vigorous disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere, along with strong winds, led to the development of multiple lines and clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, large branches and wires were reported down on Sleight-Plass Road near Poughkeepsie due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms occurred during the late afternoon hours of Thursday, August 19th ahead of an approaching cold front and upper level disturbance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Avon Place in Albany due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding and flooding that occurred during the morning of August 4th was the result of two rounds of heavy rainfall. The first heavy rainfall event occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of August 2nd, with another bout of heavy rainfall that occurred during the night of August 3rd. Over 5 inches of rain fell over the two day period that resulted in flooding during the early morning hours of August 4th along the north slopes of the Adirondacks in the North Branch of the Great Chazy drainage basin in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two day rainfall totals in excess of 5 inches, ending the morning of August 4th, fell over steep terrain of the northern slopes of the Adirondacks on Ellenburg Mountain and Spruce Hill. Numerous roads were covered by rushing water and made impassable. In addition, there was widespread damage to road surfaces in the towns of Ellenburg and Altona. One home was flooded when debris moving down a stream bed caught on a small bridge, which created a dam that diverted the flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system moved from the eastern Great Lakes region across southern New York on the 22nd and 23rd, and interacted with very moist air across the portions of St. Lawrence and Franklin counties. Widespread moderate to heavy rainfall developed by midday on the 22nd, and continued through the afternoon and evening. Widespread rainfall of 2 to 4 inches with localized 4 to 7 inches of rainfall caused flooding the night of August 22nd into the morning of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed in Stockholm, Potsdam, Norwood, and Ogdensburg New York. The worst damage was on County Rte 47 in Stockholm, between State Routes 11 and 11B, where the road side and guard rail was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached central New York from the west, warm temperatures and high humidity combined to produce instability in the presence of strong winds in the lower and middle atmosphere. These ingredients led to a round of severe thunderstorms over the area during the early and mid afternoon hours. In addition to reports of damaging winds and large hail, there were also a few funnel clouds spotted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down, with roof damage to a home. Damage occurred on Route 69 between Lee and Taberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and strong upper level trough moved across the Tri-State, triggering severe thunderstorms across Southeast New York during the afternoon.  This included both supercells and squall lines, producing severe winds and hail across the Lower Hudson Valley, New York City, and Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb fell on a 7 year old boy in Lindenhurst, causing a head injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front in conjunction with strong upper level winds produced numerous severe thunderstorms, including tornadic cells, across the New York City Metropolitan Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed at the intersection of Newport Avenue and Braxton Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved northeastward toward northern Pennsylvania, and through the region on the 25th. A surge of deep moisture associated with this system produced a period of heavy rain from late on the 24th, through the morning hours on the 25th. In addition, mild temperatures combined with the rain to melt an existing snow-pack to cause isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in the Binghamton area, and a few were closed due to flash flooding. The ramp from Interstate 81 South to Route 17 West was partially closed due to a landslide off Prospect Mountain.

Strong southerly flow allowed for the transport of tropical moisture, including the remnants of Tropical Storm Nicole, up the coast which resulted in heavy rain and flooding in portions of New York City, Nassau and Rockland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The FDR Drive was closed northbound at 61st St. due to flooding.
